title: Italian meringue frosting
subtitle: Cooked sugar syrup whisked into egg whites for a stable, fluffy finish
excerpt: Italian meringue frosting is made by pouring a hot sugar syrup at 121°C into stiff egg whites while the mixer runs. The result is a marshmallow-like frosting that holds its shape for hours, does not weep, and can be toasted with a kitchen torch for a browned-meringue effect. It is the most stable of the meringue frostings.
sourceNotes: Italian meringue (meringue italienne) is a standard confectionery technique documented in French patisserie manuals from the nineteenth century. The use of a cooked sugar syrup poured into beaten egg whites to produce a stable foam is described in Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), and in earlier French domestic science works. The method appears in English-language baking references from the early twentieth century onward. As a technique relying on standard ingredients and equipment, it is fully public-domain and widely documented in domestic baking guides.

content.text: Italian meringue frosting is made by pouring hot sugar syrup into whipped egg whites. It gives you a firm, marshmallow-smooth frosting that holds its shape for hours and takes a kitchen torch well. The cooked syrup is what makes it stable: it sets the whites so the frosting does not weep.

items.name: Egg whites
items.prepNote: at room temperature, from large eggs

content.text: Cook syrup and whip whites simultaneously

Combine the {{caster-sugar}} and {{water}} in a small saucepan over medium heat. Clip on the thermometer. When the syrup reaches 115°C, begin whipping the {{egg-whites}} with the {{cream-of-tartar}} on medium speed. When the syrup reaches 121°C (the
stage), the whites should be at soft peaks. Remove the syrup from the heat.

With the mixer running on medium speed, pour the hot syrup in a thin, steady stream down the inside of the bowl, avoiding the whisk. Increase to high speed and whip for 5 to 7 minutes until the meringue is glossy, holds stiff peaks, and the bowl feels cool at the base.

Spread or pipe immediately. For a browned effect, use a kitchen torch held 5 cm from the surface; move it constantly to avoid burning. Italian meringue takes a torch beautifully, the sugar in the syrup caramelises at the surface while the interior stays soft.

recipe.yieldDescription: enough to frost a 2-layer 20 cm cake or 12 cupcakes
